Definition: An intelligent threat detection service — continuously analyzing CloudTrail management/data events, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, EKS audit logs, RDS login activity, Lambda network activity, and S3 data events using ML. Produces severity-rated findings for compromised instances, unauthorized access, cryptocurrency mining, and data exfiltration.

EC2 findings (backdoor, cryptocurrency, trojan), IAM findings (anomalous API calls, credential exfiltration), S3 findings (unusual data access, policy changes), EKS findings (privilege escalation, anonymous access), RDS findings (anomalous login attempts), and Lambda findings (suspicious network connections).

Centralized threat detection via Organizations — delegated administrator account manages all member accounts, auto-enable for new accounts, centralized findings aggregation, and suppression rules to filter expected behavior across the organization.

A security investigation service — automatically collecting and analyzing log data (VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, GuardDuty findings, EKS audit logs) to build a behavior graph. Visualizes resource interactions, API call patterns, and entity relationships for root cause analysis.

A centralized security posture management service — aggregating findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, Firewall Manager, Config, IAM Access Analyzer, and third-party tools in ASFF format. Automated compliance checks against CIS, PCI DSS, AWS FSBP, and NIST standards.

Custom actions triggering EventBridge rules for automated remediation — e.g., auto-remediating public S3 buckets, revoking overly permissive security groups, or isolating compromised instances. Supports cross-account aggregation via Organizations delegated administrator.

An automated vulnerability management service — continuously scanning EC2 instances (via SSM Agent), Lambda functions, and ECR container images for software vulnerabilities (CVEs), network reachability issues, and package vulnerabilities with severity-rated findings and remediation guidance.

The primary API audit service — Management events (control plane operations, free first trail), Data events (S3 object-level, Lambda invocations, DynamoDB, paid), and Insights events (unusual API activity detection). Organization trails for centralized logging across all accounts.

Validating that log files haven't been tampered with — digest files (hourly SHA-256 hashes) enable validation via AWS CLI. Store logs in a dedicated security account S3 bucket with Object Lock (WORM), bucket policy denying delete, and MFA Delete enabled.

A managed data lake for CloudTrail events — enabling SQL-based querying of events across accounts and regions without setting up S3/Athena pipelines. Supports event data stores with configurable retention (up to 7 years) and integration with CloudTrail Insights.

Capturing network traffic metadata — source/destination IPs, ports, protocol, action (ACCEPT/REJECT), bytes, packets. Analyze with CloudWatch Logs Insights or Athena for detecting port scanning, data exfiltration, unusual traffic patterns, and unauthorized connection attempts.

Isolation steps — change security group to deny all traffic (forensic SG), detach from Auto Scaling, create EBS snapshots for forensic analysis, capture memory dump, enable termination protection, tag as compromised, and investigate via CloudTrail/VPC Flow Logs/GuardDuty findings.

Response steps — disable/delete compromised access keys, revoke active sessions (inline deny policy with aws:TokenIssueTime condition), review CloudTrail for unauthorized actions, check for persistence (new users, roles, policies), and rotate all potentially affected credentials.

Response steps — identify affected objects via CloudTrail data events, check S3 access logs, review bucket policy and ACL changes, enable/verify S3 Block Public Access, check Macie findings for sensitive data exposure, and assess data classification and notification requirements.

Notifications from AWS Trust & Safety about potentially compromised resources — spam, port scanning, DoS, intrusion attempts, malware hosting, or copyright violations. Must respond within 24 hours with remediation actions or risk account suspension.

Permitted without approval for 8 services — EC2, RDS, Aurora, CloudFront, API Gateway, Lambda, Lightsail, and Elastic Beanstalk. Prohibited: DNS zone walking, DoS/DDoS, port flooding, and protocol flooding. Must follow AWS Customer Support Policy for Penetration Testing.

Aggregating security logs — CloudTrail organization trail to central S3, VPC Flow Logs to central S3/CloudWatch, Config aggregator, GuardDuty delegated admin, Security Hub delegated admin, and CloudWatch Logs cross-account subscriptions via Kinesis Data Firehose.

Server access logging recording requests to an S3 bucket — capturing requester, bucket name, request time, action, response status, and error codes. Delivered to a target bucket (different from source) for audit trails. Use CloudTrail data events for more detailed API-level logging.

Monitoring security-relevant metrics — failed console sign-ins, root account usage, IAM policy changes, unauthorized API calls, security group changes, NACL changes, VPC changes, and CloudTrail configuration changes. Use metric filters on CloudTrail logs with SNS notifications.

Extracting metric data from log events — creating custom CloudWatch metrics from patterns in CloudTrail logs (e.g., count of ConsoleLogin failures, root account API calls, unauthorized API calls). Trigger alarms and automated responses via SNS and Lambda.

Event-driven security automation — triggering Lambda/SSM Automation on AWS API events like IAM policy changes, security group modifications, S3 bucket policy changes, root account login, and Config rule compliance changes for real-time remediation.

Continuous compliance evaluation — managed rules for security (restricted-ssh, encrypted-volumes, s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited, iam-password-policy, root-account-mfa-enabled). Conformance packs for frameworks (CIS, PCI DSS). Auto-remediation via SSM Automation documents.

A data security service using ML — automatically discovering, classifying, and protecting sensitive data in S3 (PII, financial data, credentials, PHI). Provides data inventory, sensitivity scoring, automated discovery jobs, and findings integrated with Security Hub and EventBridge.

Sensitive data findings (specific data types detected) and policy findings (bucket-level issues like public access, no encryption). Automate responses via EventBridge — quarantine objects, notify teams, apply encryption, or restrict bucket access.

Personal Health Dashboard events routed to EventBridge — triggering automated responses to AWS operational issues affecting your resources (scheduled maintenance, service degradation, account notifications). Enables proactive security incident awareness.

Query logging for DNS requests — capturing domain names queried, source IPs, response codes, and Route 53 Resolver endpoints. Sent to CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Kinesis Data Firehose for detecting DNS tunneling, data exfiltration via DNS, and malicious domain lookups.

Detailed request logs for ALB/NLB/CLB — capturing client IP, latencies, request paths, server responses, and TLS cipher details. Stored in S3 for security analysis, identifying attack patterns, and troubleshooting. Enable for compliance and forensic readiness.

Ingesting security logs (CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, WAF logs, GuardDuty findings) via Kinesis Data Firehose — enabling real-time search, visualization with OpenSearch Dashboards, anomaly detection, and alerting for security operations center (SOC) workflows.

Continuous audit evidence collection — mapping to compliance frameworks (SOC 2, PCI DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, FedRAMP). Automatically collects evidence from Config, CloudTrail, Security Hub, and manual sources. Generates assessment reports for auditors.

Securing log data — S3 Object Lock (WORM) for immutability, SSE-KMS encryption, bucket policies denying delete/overwrite, MFA Delete, CloudTrail log file integrity validation (digest files), cross-account log delivery, and versioning for tamper evidence.

Defense in depth — public/private subnet separation, security groups (stateful, instance-level), NACLs (stateless, subnet-level), VPC Flow Logs, NAT Gateway for private outbound, VPC endpoints for private AWS access, and Network Firewall for advanced inspection.

Security Groups: stateful, allow rules only, all rules evaluated, instance/ENI level, reference other SGs. NACLs: stateless, allow+deny rules, evaluated in numbered order, subnet level, return traffic must be explicitly allowed. Use both for defense in depth.

Layer 7 protection for CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync, and Cognito — Web ACLs with rules for SQL injection, XSS, rate limiting, IP/geo blocking, bot control, and account takeover prevention. AWS Managed Rules, custom rules, and regex pattern sets.

Term: WAF Rule Groups and Managed Rules
Definition: AWS Managed Rules: Core Rule Set (CRS), Known Bad Inputs, SQL Database, Linux/Windows OS, PHP/WordPress, Bot Control, Account Takeover Prevention (ATP). Custom rule groups for application-specific logic. Rule priority determines evaluation order within Web ACLs.

Full request logging to S3, CloudWatch Logs, or Kinesis Data Firehose — capturing matched rules, actions taken, request headers, and source IPs. CloudWatch metrics for allowed/blocked/counted requests. Use for tuning rules and identifying attack patterns.

DDoS protection — Shield Standard (free, automatic Layer 3/4 protection for all resources) and Shield Advanced ($3,000/month, enhanced protection for CloudFront/ALB/NLB/EIP/Global Accelerator/Route 53 with 24/7 DDoS Response Team, cost protection, and WAF included).

Real-time attack visibility, automatic application-layer DDoS mitigation (WAF rules), health-based detection, proactive engagement from DDoS Response Team (DRT), cost protection (credits for scaling during attacks), and global threat environment dashboard.

Centralized security policy management across Organizations — managing WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, VPC security groups, Network Firewall policies, Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rules, and third-party firewalls. Auto-applies to new accounts/resources.

A managed stateful/stateless firewall for VPCs — Suricata-compatible IPS/IDS rules, domain-based filtering (allow/deny lists), TLS inspection (decrypt and inspect HTTPS), protocol detection, and centralized management via Firewall Manager.

Filtering outbound DNS queries from VPCs — blocking domains associated with malware, botnets, and data exfiltration. Managed domain lists (AWS) and custom domain lists. Actions: ALLOW, BLOCK (with custom response), ALERT. Managed via Firewall Manager.

Gateway endpoints (S3, DynamoDB): route table entries, endpoint policies for access control. Interface endpoints (PrivateLink): ENI with security groups, endpoint policies, private DNS. Both keep traffic on AWS network — use endpoint policies to restrict which resources/actions are accessible.

Resource-based policies controlling access through VPC endpoints — restricting which S3 buckets, DynamoDB tables, or API actions are accessible via the endpoint. Combined with S3 bucket policies using aws:sourceVpce condition to enforce VPC-only access.

Private connectivity between VPCs and services — service provider exposes via NLB + endpoint service, consumer creates interface endpoint. Traffic never traverses internet, supports cross-account, and is used for SaaS integrations and internal service exposure.

Origin Access Control (OAC) for S3, signed URLs/cookies for private content, field-level encryption (encrypt specific POST fields at edge), geo-restriction, HTTPS enforcement (viewer/origin protocol policies), custom SSL certificates via ACM, and WAF integration.

Term: CloudFront Signed URLs vs Signed Cookies
Definition: Signed URLs: individual file access, RTMP streaming, client doesn't support cookies. Signed Cookies: multiple files (entire site), don't want to change URLs. Both use CloudFront key pairs (trusted key groups) with expiration time, IP restrictions, and path patterns.

IMDSv2 (require token-based metadata access), disable IMDS if not needed, security groups (least privilege), Systems Manager for patch management, Inspector for vulnerability scanning, EBS encryption, and instance profile roles instead of embedded credentials.

IMDSv1: simple HTTP GET, vulnerable to SSRF attacks (attacker can steal IAM credentials from metadata). IMDSv2: requires PUT request for session token first, token has TTL, X-Forwarded-For header blocked — mitigates SSRF. Best practice: enforce IMDSv2 via launch template HttpTokens=required.

Patch Manager (automated patching), Session Manager (no SSH/bastion, audit logging), Run Command (remote execution without open ports), State Manager (enforce configurations), and Inventory (track installed software). All with IAM-based access control and CloudTrail logging.

ECR image scanning (on push, continuous), ECS/EKS task/pod IAM roles (least privilege), Fargate for isolation, Secrets Manager for sensitive config, Network Policies (EKS), Security Groups for pods (EKS), and runtime monitoring with GuardDuty EKS Protection.

Evaluation order: explicit Deny (any policy) → Organizations SCPs → Resource-based policies → Identity-based policies → Permission boundaries → Session policies. Explicit Deny always wins. All policies must allow for cross-account access (except resource-based policies which can grant directly).

Identity-based (attached to users/groups/roles), Resource-based (attached to resources — S3, SQS, KMS, Lambda), Permission boundaries (maximum permissions cap for IAM entities), SCPs (organization-wide guardrails), Session policies (temporary session restrictions), and ACLs (legacy, S3/VPC).

Fine-grained access control — aws:SourceIp (IP restriction), aws:SourceVpc/aws:SourceVpce (VPC restriction), aws:PrincipalOrgID (organization restriction), aws:RequestedRegion (region restriction), aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent (MFA requirement), and aws:TagKeys (tag-based access).

EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution roles, ECS task roles, EKS IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), and service-linked roles. Best practice: use roles instead of access keys, apply least privilege, and use aws:SourceArn/aws:SourceAccount conditions to prevent confused deputy.

IAM role assumption (trust policy + permissions policy), resource-based policies (S3, KMS, SQS, SNS, Lambda), RAM for resource sharing, and Organizations delegated administrator. For human access: IAM Identity Center with permission sets mapped to accounts.

Issuing temporary credentials — AssumeRole (cross-account, service roles), AssumeRoleWithSAML (SAML federation), AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity (web/mobile federation via Cognito), GetSessionToken (MFA-protected API access), and GetFederationToken (custom federation broker).

Identifying unintended resource access — analyzing S3 buckets, IAM roles, KMS keys, Lambda functions, SQS queues, and Secrets Manager secrets for external access. Policy validation against best practices, policy generation from CloudTrail activity, and unused access analysis.

Maximum permissions cap for IAM users and roles — the effective permissions are the intersection of identity-based policies AND the permission boundary. Used for delegated administration: allow developers to create roles but only within defined boundaries.

User Pools: MFA (SMS, TOTP, email), adaptive authentication (risk-based challenges), compromised credential detection, advanced security features (IP-based throttling), and custom authentication flows (Lambda triggers). Identity Pools: fine-grained IAM roles for authenticated/unauthenticated users.

Centralized workforce access — permission sets (collections of IAM policies) assigned to users/groups for specific accounts, integrating with AD/external IdPs, ABAC (attribute-based access control), and automatic provisioning via SCIM. Replaces per-account IAM user management.

SAML 2.0 (enterprise IdP → IAM roles or Identity Center), OIDC/OAuth (web/mobile → Cognito Identity Pools → IAM roles), Custom Identity Broker (legacy → STS AssumeRole/GetFederationToken), and AD Connector/AWS Managed AD for Active Directory integration.

AWS Managed Microsoft AD (full AD in AWS, trust relationships with on-premises), AD Connector (proxy to on-premises AD, no caching), Simple AD (Samba-based, standalone, small/medium). Choose Managed AD for AWS service integration; AD Connector for existing on-premises AD.

Enable MFA (hardware MFA preferred), don't create access keys, use for account-level tasks only (close account, change support plan, enable MFA), monitor with CloudTrail + CloudWatch alarm + EventBridge rule, and store credentials securely with break-glass procedures.

Organization-level guardrails — restrict actions across member accounts (don't affect management account). Common patterns: deny region usage, deny leaving organization, require encryption, deny public S3, require specific tags, and deny root account usage except for specific actions.

A security issue where a trusted service is tricked into acting on behalf of an unauthorized entity — mitigate with aws:SourceArn and aws:SourceAccount conditions in trust policies and resource-based policies to verify the requesting service's identity.

Start with minimal permissions, use IAM Access Analyzer to generate policies from activity, review with policy simulator, implement permission boundaries for delegation, use SCPs for organization guardrails, and regularly audit with credential reports and access advisor.

Managed encryption key service — symmetric (AES-256) and asymmetric (RSA, ECC) keys, key policies (resource-based), grants (temporary access), automatic annual rotation, envelope encryption (data key encrypts data, CMK encrypts data key), and CloudTrail audit of all key usage.

AWS owned keys (free, AWS manages, no visibility), AWS managed keys (aws/service, free, auto-rotation, CloudTrail logging), Customer managed keys (full control, key policy, rotation, cross-account, $1/month + API charges). Choose customer managed for audit requirements and cross-account sharing.

Resource-based policies controlling key access — default policy (root account full access), key administrators (manage but not use), key users (encrypt/decrypt), and grants (temporary, programmatic access). Key policy is the primary access control mechanism; IAM policies alone are insufficient without key policy allowing.

Two-tier encryption — GenerateDataKey returns plaintext + encrypted data key, application encrypts data with plaintext key, stores encrypted data + encrypted data key together. Decrypt: KMS decrypts the data key, application decrypts data. Efficient for large data without sending to KMS.

Replica keys in multiple regions with the same key material — enabling cross-region encrypt/decrypt without cross-region API calls. Use for DynamoDB Global Tables encryption, S3 cross-region replication with encryption, and disaster recovery scenarios.

Dedicated single-tenant HSMs — FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated, you manage keys (AWS has no access), supports symmetric/asymmetric keys, digital signing, and SSL/TLS offloading. Use for regulatory requirements mandating dedicated hardware or when you need full key control.

KMS: multi-tenant, AWS manages HSM, integrated with 100+ services, automatic rotation, lower cost, FIPS 140-2 Level 2. CloudHSM: single-tenant, you manage keys, FIPS 140-2 Level 3, custom key store, supports PKCS#11/JCE/CNG. Use CloudHSM for strict compliance or custom cryptographic operations.

Bridging KMS and CloudHSM — KMS CMKs backed by CloudHSM cluster keys, providing KMS API convenience with CloudHSM-level control. Use when you need KMS integration with AWS services but require keys stored in dedicated HSMs for compliance.

Server-side: SSE-S3 (AES-256, default, Amazon-managed), SSE-KMS (KMS keys, audit trail, separate permissions, per-request charges, S3 Bucket Keys reduce KMS costs), SSE-C (customer-provided keys, HTTPS required). Client-side: encrypt before upload with KMS or custom keys.

Reducing KMS API costs for SSE-KMS — generating a bucket-level key that creates data keys for objects, reducing KMS requests by up to 99%. The bucket key is cached and reused, with CloudTrail showing bucket ARN instead of object ARN for KMS events.

WORM (Write Once Read Many) protection — Governance mode (users with special permissions can override), Compliance mode (no one can delete, including root, until retention expires), and Legal Hold (indefinite protection until explicitly removed). Requires versioning enabled.

Four settings preventing public access — BlockPublicAcls (block new public ACLs), IgnorePublicAcls (ignore existing public ACLs), BlockPublicPolicy (block new public bucket policies), RestrictPublicBuckets (restrict public/cross-account access). Apply at account level for organization-wide protection.

AES-256 encryption using KMS keys — encrypts data at rest, in transit (between instance and volume), snapshots, and volumes created from snapshots. Default encryption setting per region. Cannot directly encrypt an unencrypted volume — create encrypted snapshot copy, then new volume.

Encryption at rest using KMS — must be enabled at creation (cannot encrypt existing unencrypted DB). Encrypts storage, automated backups, read replicas, and snapshots. To encrypt existing DB: create snapshot → copy with encryption → restore from encrypted snapshot.

Storing and rotating secrets — automatic rotation via Lambda for RDS/Aurora/Redshift/DocumentDB, cross-account sharing via resource policies, versioning (AWSCURRENT/AWSPREVIOUS), and CloudTrail logging of all access. Use for database credentials, API keys, and OAuth tokens.

Secrets Manager: native rotation, cross-account, higher cost ($0.40/secret/month), designed for secrets. Parameter Store: free standard tier (up to 10,000), hierarchical, no native rotation (use Lambda), simpler. Use Secrets Manager for DB credentials needing rotation; Parameter Store for configuration.

Managing SSL/TLS certificates — free public certificates with auto-renewal for ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway, NLB. Private CA for internal certificates ($400/month). DNS validation (preferred, automatic renewal) or email validation. Cannot export public certificates.

Issuing private certificates for internal resources — TLS for internal services, IoT device certificates, code signing, and document signing. Supports certificate revocation via CRL and OCSP. Hierarchical CA structure with root and subordinate CAs.

Identify (Macie for S3, Tag-based classification), Protect (KMS encryption, S3 Block Public Access, VPC endpoints), Detect (GuardDuty, Config rules, Security Hub), Respond (EventBridge automation, Lambda remediation), and Recover (backups, versioning, cross-region replication).

TLS/SSL for data in motion — ACM certificates for ALB/CloudFront/API Gateway, VPN encryption for hybrid, PrivateLink for private connectivity, S3 bucket policy enforcing aws:SecureTransport, RDS SSL/TLS connections, and EBS/EFS encryption in transit between instance and storage.

Multi-account security strategy — SCPs for permission guardrails, dedicated security account (GuardDuty/Security Hub delegated admin), log archive account (CloudTrail/Config), network account (Transit Gateway/Firewall), and Control Tower for automated governance.

Automated landing zone setup — mandatory guardrails (CloudTrail enabled, log archive protection), strongly recommended guardrails (EBS encryption, RDS encryption), elective guardrails (S3 versioning). Account Factory for standardized provisioning with pre-configured VPCs and baselines.

Continuous compliance — managed rules (encrypted-volumes, restricted-ssh, s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only, iam-root-access-key-check), conformance packs (CIS, PCI DSS, NIST), organization-wide Config via aggregator, and auto-remediation via SSM Automation documents.

Automatic and manual remediation — SSM Automation documents triggered by non-compliant Config rule evaluations. Examples: auto-enable S3 encryption, auto-enable EBS encryption, auto-revoke public security group rules, and auto-enable CloudTrail logging.

IaC security — stack policies (prevent accidental updates to critical resources), drift detection (identify manual changes), StackSets with Organizations (deploy security baselines across accounts), and cfn-guard/cfn-nag for template security validation before deployment.

Controlled resource provisioning — portfolios with approved CloudFormation products, launch constraints (IAM role for provisioning), TagOptions for mandatory tags, and sharing across accounts via Organizations. Ensures users deploy only pre-approved, compliant architectures.

Tracking software licenses — managing BYOL (Bring Your Own License) compliance for Windows, Oracle, SQL Server on EC2 and Dedicated Hosts. License rules enforce limits, and automated discovery tracks usage against entitlements to prevent compliance violations.

A self-service portal for AWS compliance reports and agreements — accessing SOC reports, PCI DSS attestation, ISO certifications, HIPAA BAA, and other compliance documents. Organization agreements apply to all member accounts.

EventBridge + Lambda for real-time remediation, Config rules + SSM Automation for compliance enforcement, GuardDuty → Security Hub → EventBridge → Lambda for threat response, and CloudFormation Guard for preventive infrastructure validation.

Security checks — open ports (0.0.0.0/0), IAM use, MFA on root, S3 bucket permissions, CloudTrail logging, EBS/RDS public snapshots, and exposed access keys. Full checks require Business/Enterprise Support. Integrate with EventBridge for automated responses.

CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark (Security Hub), PCI DSS (Config conformance pack), HIPAA (BAA via Artifact, eligible services), SOC 1/2/3 (Artifact reports), FedRAMP (GovCloud), GDPR (data residency, encryption, Macie), and ISO 27001 (Artifact certification).

Seven design principles — implement strong identity foundation, enable traceability, apply security at all layers, automate security best practices, protect data in transit and at rest, keep people away from data, and prepare for security events.
